summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the Court had found a financial agreement between the parties was not binding under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) – Where there was no dispute the parties were in a de facto relationship – Property alteration pursuant to s 90SM of the Act – Where the balance sheet of the parties’ assets was agreed – Where the applicant de facto wife (“wife”) sought overall property division 35/65 in favour of the respondent de facto husband (“husband”) – Where the husband sought the sale of one real property with proceeds split equally between the parties – Where the wife sought to retain the property as her principal place of residence – Where the wife failed to establish claims of family violence – Where the Court was not satisfied the wife gave up a valuable group of businesses to move to Australia with the husband – Where the husband is retired and used income from a commercial partnership to fund the parties’ lifestyle and extensive travel – Where the husband made overwhelming financial contributions and the wife made greater non-financial contributions – Contributions assessed 12/88 in favour of the husband – Adjustment of 4 per cent in the wife’s favour pursuant to s 90SM(5) of the Act – Final division 16/84 in favour of the husband – Orders for the husband to pay an adjustment sum and transfer one real property to the wife.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARPER J:

INTRODUCTION

These are property proceedings under Pt VIIIAB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) between the applicant de facto wife, Ms Ericsson (“wife”) and the respondent de facto husband, Mr Rogers (“husband”).
There was no dispute the parties were in a de facto relationship as defined by s 4AA of the Act. The parties agreed they commenced a de facto relationship in December 2007, separated for a short period around 2012, reconciled in early 2013 and then separated on a final basis in March 2022. The relationship was approximately 14 years in duration.
There are no children of the relationship. Both parties have adult children from previous relationships.
The parties agreed the net asset pool is $7,228,062.
On 3 July 2025, the Court declared a financial agreement entered into by the parties in March 2013 was not binding within the meaning of s 90UJ of the Act (Ericsson & Rogers [2025] FedCFamC1F 445 (“Ericsson & Rogers”)). Consequently, the jurisdiction of the Court to order a division of property under Pt VIIIAB is not excluded.
By way of Amended Initiating Application filed 26 February 2026, the wife seeks orders for property division under s 90SM of the Act. In final submissions she amended her orders to claim a lump sum payment of $1,785,000 from the husband and the transfer by the husband to her of a property at F Street, City G State H, USA (“[City G]”), which is her principal place of residence. The City G property is registered in the husband’s name, but this is discussed later. The wife claims the lump sum payment, City G and her retained assets represent about 35 per cent of the value of the property pool. Her case is based on an assessment of the wife’s contributions at 25 per cent with a 10 per cent adjustment for future needs, for an overall property division 35/65 in favour of the husband.
The husband in his Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 27 February 2026, seeks the City G property be sold with net proceeds split equally between the parties, and that the parties otherwise retain the assets and liabilities in their own names. His case is based on an assessment of contributions 3/97 per cent in his favour with an adjustment of about 3 per cent to the wife for future needs, giving an overall property division of 6.5/93.5 in his favour.
Both parties made submissions about the credit of the other party. In my view neither party presented as a totally reliable witness, but overall they both tried to answer questions as best they could. In any event, as the following reasons show, it is not necessary to express a view about the credit of the parties in this case.

BACKGROUND

The wife was born in 1961 and is 64 years of age. She resides at the City G property. She is currently self-employed by her company called “[D Company]”. She works part time and earns about $505 per week. She also receives US Social Security benefits of $382 per week.
The husband was born in 1951 and is 74 years of age. He retired in 2006 and resides in Town K, NSW.
In or around 1998, the husband entered into a partnership with five other individuals to develop and build a centre, named “[L Commerical Centre]”, on commercial land at M Street, Suburb N (“commercial centre”). The land at Suburb N was partly owned by the husband, and the remainder had been purchased by four of the partnership members from a third party. The husband and his former wife, Ms O, each held a 36.5 per cent share in the partnership.
In 2014, Ms O sold her interest, and the husband purchased 3.5 per cent of her share to bring his total interest to 40 per cent of the partnership. At the date of trial, the partnership was comprised of four individuals with the husband retaining his 40 per cent interest. The partnership has a debt to ANZ of $2,250,000 through a commercial bill, of which the husband has a one-third liability for $750,000. He states he pays $4,400 per month to service the commercial bill. Since retirement, the husband’s primary income is from his interest in the commercial centre. At the date of trial he receives about $6,796 per week.
There was no dispute that the wife has no interest in the commercial centre and made no financial or other direct contribution to its operation or management.
In 1998, the wife founded a chain of drive-through outlets under the umbrella brand name “[P Pty Ltd Entity 2]” across State Q and State R, USA. The chain grew to a number of outlets either owned or licensed by the wife to others. The business of the outlets was conducted through a corporation called P Pty Ltd Entity 1. This company also owned the property upon which one of the outlets was operated.
In March 2007, the parties met through an online dating website. At the time the wife was living in the USA and the husband was living in Australia. Both parties were separated from their previous spouses, but neither had finalised property settlement proceedings.
In or around May 2007, the husband transferred $4,000 to the wife for payment of her bills.
In June 2007, the husband travelled to the USA to meet the wife in person for the first time. The parties spent about four weeks together in State Q before the husband returned to Australia.
Between June to December 2007, the husband claimed he transferred about USD20,000 to the wife for various bills and expenses. The wife conceded this specific amount in cross examination and accepted the husband paid for some, not all, of the bills for her business, rent for an apartment, for her shop door to be repaired and for her flight and possessions to be shipped to Australia.
In November 2007, the wife transferred her ownership of most of the drive-through outlets, the franchise application and fees, and her equity in some real estate to her previous husband as part of finalising their divorce. She made claims the respondent husband hurried her to finalise the divorce and that she gave away a valuable business in reliance on his promises to financially support her. The husband questioned the value and level of debt held by the wife's business, he suggested it was to her benefit to offload the business to her former husband.
In December 2007, the parties commenced cohabitation in Australia at the husband’s home at S Street, Suburb T (“[Suburb T]”). At the time, the property was jointly owned by the husband and his former wife, Ms O.
Between late 2007 and March 2008, the wife sold her remaining drive-through outlet, P Pty Ltd Entity 2 in City U, and its parcel of commercial real estate, and after payment of taxes and repayment of a mortgage, USD9,500 remained. She transferred this sum to the husband’s NAB account.
In or around late 2009, the husband sold Suburb T for $915,000, with net proceeds of $875,000 of which $675,000 was paid to Ms O as part of a property settlement. The husband retained approximately $200,000.
In or around late 2009, the parties purchased a property at J Street, Town K (“[Town K]”) as joint tenants for about $440,000 plus stamp duty and other fees. The purchase was funded by the husband from his savings and proceeds from the sale of Suburb T. The wife did not make a financial contribution to the purchase. The parties moved into the property after settlement.
In or around early 2010, the parties purchased a 50 per cent share in a motorhome, with the other half share owned by friends in State V, USA. The husband claimed the half share cost USD30,000, but he transferred USD59,618 from his NAB cheque account to an account in the wife’s name with W Bank, the additional funds being for living expenses of the parties.
In 2012, whilst the parties were in State V, the wife’s former husband died and she went to visit and comfort her daughter. According to the wife her visit led to the parties having an argument. Both parties claimed the other initiated a separation at this time.
In October 2012, the parties reached an agreement about the manner in which they should divide their assets after termination of their relationship. The wife executed a document to transfer her interest in Town K to the husband in exchange for payment of USD150,000. The wife made claims she signed the document under duress or coercion. But she conceded in cross examination that the parties had reached a mutually agreeable position and that is why she emailed a solicitor with instructions about the agreement days prior to signing the document.
Between November and December 2012, the wife moved out of the Town K property and returned to live in the USA.
In early 2013, the parties reconciled and the wife returned to Australia.
In mid-2013, City G was purchased for USD189,499. The property was registered in the wife’s name. The parties gave divergent accounts of how the purchase was financed.
According to the wife, she contributed USD150,000, with the balance of the purchase price funded by the husband in exchange for USD42,000 she held in her US retirement account (“401(k) account”). She claims the husband agreed to pay all property expenses as his friends and family would also use the property.
The husband claims he entirely funded the purchase through the transfer of USD90,960 to the wife, and with the balance of the USD150,000 that he transferred to the wife in 2012 when the parties separated. He denies the wife’s 401(k) account funds were used to purchase the property, but rather the parties agreed later to withdraw about USD32,000 from that account for everyday living expenses due to the exchange rates at the time.
In cross examination the wife accepted the husband transferred USD90,960 to her, but claimed only USD40,000 was used to complete the purchase, with the balance applied to furnishings and other household items necessary to “set up” City G as accommodation. This account seems more likely. The wife claimed in cross examination, and it seemed undisputed, that she held about USD42,000 in her 401(k) account at the date of purchase, but she claimed she only withdrew amounts from that account beginning in 2016 until it was depleted by 2019. This is more consistent with the wife’s version of the purchase of City G.
On 23 May 2014, the wife transferred 50 per cent of the City G property to the husband as a tenant in common. She did not receive financial consideration from the husband for the transfer. She claims he told her he needed to be on title to a USA property to obtain an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (“ITIN”), required for taxation in the USA, and promised to return his share of City G to her once he received his ITIN. In cross examination she conceded she wanted the husband to own a share of City G so he could spend longer living in the USA, and that at the time, she did not think it mattered who was on title because the parties were in a serious relationship.
The husband argued the property had only been registered in the wife’s name because she was a US citizen. He claimed he received inaccurate advice that the property could not be put in his name, but when this was corrected the parties agreed the wife would transfer half of her interest to the husband.
In 2015, the wife underwent surgery in Australia.
In 2017, the husband was diagnosed with cancer. He has required significant medical treatment.
On 24 July 2019, the wife transferred her remaining 50 per cent share of City G to the husband. She did not receive financial consideration from the husband for the transfer. She claims he told her he needed to hold the whole title to City G to obtain an ITIN and promised to return the title to her upon obtaining the ITIN.
The husband argued it was the wife’s idea to transfer her remaining interest in City G to him because her adult children were pressuring her in some way for money. The wife denied this. In cross examination the husband accepted that he still considered the wife to be a co-owner of City G.
In or around 2019 and 2021, the husband paid for the wife to attend two courses.
Throughout the relationship the parties travelled frequently to the USA and other international destinations together. They spent several months in the USA most years.
In March 2022, the parties separated on a final basis. The husband transferred to the wife USD24,000 for living expenses.
In or around May 2022, the wife returned to live at City G and has resided there since.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

Some procedural history is relevant.
On 12 March 2024, the wife filed an Initiating Application seeking financial orders in Division 2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia. She sought a division of property that included payment from the husband to the wife of $200,000, that she retain City G and that she receive a percentage of sale proceeds from the husband’s interest in the commercial centre.
On 17 May 2024, the husband filed a Response to Initiating Application seeking the wife’s application be dismissed on claims the parties had entered into a Binding Financial Agreement (“BFA”).
On 25 February 2025, the matter was transferred to Division 1.
On 3 July 2025, as mentioned, the Court declared the financial agreement entered into by the parties in March 2013 was not binding under the Act.
On 16 January 2026, by way of consent, the husband was ordered to pay the wife the sum of $150,000 categorised as interim property distribution, and $40,000 for costs of the interim hearing. The husband paid $190,000 to the wife on 24 February 2026 and as at the date of trial, $150,000 remained in the wife’s solicitor’s trust account.
On 26 February 2026, the wife filed an Amended Initiating Application.
On 27 February 2026, the husband filed an Amended Response to Initiating Application.
The final hearing commenced on 9 March 2026 and ran for three days with judgment reserved on 11 March 2026.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The wife relied upon the following documents:
(a) Case Outline filed 6 March 2026;

(b) Amended Initiating Application filed 26 February 2026;

(c) Affidavit of the wife filed 4 March 2026 (and Annexures MSE-1, MSE-2, MSE-7, MSE-8, MSE-11, MSE-12 and MSE-13);

(d) Financial Statement filed 4 March 2026; and

(e) Annexures MRR-19 and MRR-34 of the husband’s affidavit filed 4 March 2026.

The wife was cross examined.
The husband relied upon the following documents:
(a) Case Outline filed 6 March 2026;

(b) Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 27 February 2026;

(c) Affidavit of the husband filed 4 March 2026; and

(d) Financial Statement filed 4 March 2026.

The husband was cross examined.
The documents tendered and received into evidence are set out in Schedule 1 to these reasons.

Expert evidence

A number of single experts were appointed in this matter.
Ms Lynn Barnett was appointed to value the City G property. She provided a report dated 22 September 2025 valuing the property as at 15 September 2025, with supplementary answers dated 6 October 2025.
Mr Richard Perry was appointed to value the commercial centreand the Town K property. He provided a retrospective valuation report of the commercial centredated 2 October 2025, valuing the property as at December 2007; and a second report dated 30 September 2025 of the current market valuation as at 26 September 2025. For the Town K valuation, he provided a report dated 26 September 2025 valuing the property as at 19 September 2025.

PART VIIIAB

In order for the Court to have the jurisdiction to make property orders under the Act in respect of a de facto relationship, there are two jurisdictional facts that must be established on the evidence. Those jurisdictional facts are contained in s 4AA and s 90SB of the Act.
It is common ground that the parties’ relationship broke down after 2022. It is also common ground, and I accept, that this relationship fell within the definition of a “de facto relationship” within s 4AA of the Act and that it existed for at least two years. Accordingly, the Court’s jurisdiction is established.
Part VIIIAB of the Act sets out the legislative provisions relating to property orders that may be sought when parties were in a de facto relationship. The central provision is s 90SM of the Act, which gives the Court power to make such orders for alteration of property interests as it considers appropriate.
Section 90SM(1) empowers the Court to make such orders as it considers appropriate in “altering the interests of the parties to the de facto relationship” in “the property of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them”.
Section 90SM(2) of the Act provides that:
The court must not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.
Section 90SM(3) provides that:
(3) In considering what order (if any) should be made under this section in property settlement proceedings, the court:
(a) is to identify:
(i) the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them; and

(ii) the existing liabilities of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them; and
(b) is to take into account (except for the purpose of making an order with respect to the ownership of property that is a companion animal):
(i) the considerations set out in subsection (4) (considerations relating to contributions); and
(ii) the considerations set out in subsection (5) (considerations relating to current and future circumstances).
In Stanford v Stanford [2012] FamCAFC 1; (2012) 247 CLR 108 (“Stanford”) the High Court made clear at [37], when considering the pre-amendment version of the equivalent s 79, that it was necessary to begin consideration of whether it is just and equitable to make a property settlement order by identifying, according to ordinary common law and equitable principles, the existing legal and equitable interests of the parties in property, as well as their liabilities and financial resources at the date of the hearing (see Bevan & Bevan [2013] FamCAFC 116; (2013) FLC 93-545 (“Bevan”) at [72]–[73]).
Stanford at [40] also made clear that to conclude that making an order is “just and equitable” only because of and by reference to various matters in s 90SM(4) and s 90SM(5), without a separate consideration of s 90SM(2) would be to conflate the statutory requirements and ignore the principles laid down by the Act. The requirement to make an order that is just and equitable permeates the entire decision-making process and is not a threshold issue (Martin & Newton [2011] FamCAFC 233; (2011) FLC 93-490 at [306]; Bevan at [62], [86]). Here both parties accept it would be just and equitable to make some form of adjustment. However, the Court must also be satisfied that the final proposed orders are just and equitable, and I will return to this below.
If the Court is satisfied that it is just and equitable to make a property adjustment order, the Court is obliged to consider the matters set forth in s 90SM(4) and s 90SM(5).
The question of whether it is just and equitable to make an order is not to be answered by assuming that the parties’ rights or interests in marital property are or should be different from those existing at trial (Stanford at [37]–[39], [50]; Wirth v Wirth [1956] HCA 71; (1956) 98 CLR 228 at 231–232 and 247–248). There is no presumption of equal sharing of property interests or community property in Australian law (Mallet v Mallet [1984] HCA 21; (1984) 156 CLR 605 (“Mallet”)). Unless and until property rights or interests are adjusted pursuant to s 90SM, one de facto spouse has no inchoate or other proprietary interest in the exclusive property owned by the other spouse (Bevan at [80]; Lin & Ruan [2021] FamCAFC 90; (2021) FLC 94-024 at [41], [48]–[49]; Sarto & Sarto [2022] FedCFamC1A 16; (2022) 65 Fam LR 605 (“Sarto”) at [19]; Agnarsson & Agnarsson (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1F 407 at [46]; B Pty Ltd & Anker [2025] FedCFamC1A 85; (2025) 71 Fam LR 358 at [16]). It is wrong to assume that one spouse enjoys an inchoate interest in the exclusive property of the other spouse “simply because it was acquired during the marriage” (Sarto at [24]). Until the Court exercises its discretion to adjust property interests and unless restrained by an injunction made under s 114(1) of the Act, the spouses may each exert the full measure of their property rights against third parties and one another (Kartal & Templeman [2022] FedCFamC1A 46 at [53]).
In particular, where a property is purchased by one spouse entering into a mortgage, the funds derived from the mortgage loan are a payment and contribution by that spouse. Borrowed money normally (in the absence, for example, of a Quistclose trust) becomes the beneficial property of the borrower and the lender is left with an in personam right, secured or unsecured, of repayment (Twinsectra Ltd v Yardley [2002] UKHL 12; [2002] 2 AC 164 at [68]; Ying v Song [2010] NSWSC 1500 at [32]; Papas v Co [2018] NSWSC 1404 at [399]). Putting to one side situations where the other spouse may have a proprietary interest in the property under a resulting or constructive trust, the property is an asset of the spouse who is the owner, unless and until the Court exercises its power to divide the parties’ assets by adjusting the legal interests.
The Court is required to make orders which are not only “just and equitable” but also “appropriate” and s 90ST reflects a policy of making orders which finally determine the financial relationship between the parties and avoid further proceedings.

ASSETS, LIABILITIES AND FINANCIAL RESOURCES AT THE DATE OF THE HEARING

I turn then to the identification of the parties’ property, liabilities, and financial resources at the date of the hearing, according to ordinary principles of law and equity.
An agreed joint balance sheet was tendered by the parties, which became Exhibit C. There were no items in contention.
Based on Exhibit C, the asset pool is as follows (with figures rounded and excluding items with a nil or zero balance):

Consequently, if there was no property adjustment, with the percentages rounded, the husband would hold 97 per cent and the wife about 3 per cent of the net assets. As noted, both parties agreed there should be a just and equitable property adjustment, and it is therefore not appropriate to leave the assets and liabilities undisturbed.
I turn now to consider the application of Pt VIIIAB of the Act and the factors set forth in s 90SM.

CONTRIBUTIONS

Section 90SM(4) sets out the considerations relating to contributions to be taken into account by the Court in determining what order (if any) should be made under s 90SM in property settlement proceedings.
There was no dispute that I should take a global approach to the asset pool (Norbis v Norbis [1986] HCA 17; (1986) 161 CLR 513 (“Norbis”)).
In accordance with s 90SM(4) of the Act, it has been settled for many years that the Court must consider all the contributions, both financial and non-financial, to the acquisition, conservation, and improvement of the parties’ assets, as well as to the welfare of the family during cohabitation and after separation. The Court must consider the contributions in an overall sense with a broad approach rather than mathematical calculation (Norman & Norman [2010] FamCAFC 66; Kowalski and Kowalski [1992] FamCA 54; (1993) FLC 92-342; G and G (2000) FLC 93-043). Assumptions about equality of contributions should not be made (Mallet at 610, 613, 625, 635–636 and 646–647). Contributions made to the welfare of the family or as homemaker are to be taken into account substantively and not in any merely token way (Mallet at 636 per Wilson J), notwithstanding that those contributions may not be, or cannot be seen to be, directly linked to the available property at trial, or any increase or decrease in the value of the property (Dickons v Dickons [2012] FamCAFC 154; (2012) 50 Fam LR 244 at [17]–[20]). The Full Court has endorsed a holistic approach to assessing contributions (Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78; (2019) FLC 93-898 at [31]–[87]).
I have already set out above (at [9]–[42]) many of the parties’ contributions in the course of describing the background, which I have taken into account. I will not repeat myself, except where necessary. Below is a further discussion of the evidence and my findings in relation to the relevant contributions.

(a) the financial contribution made directly or indirectly by or on behalf of a party to the de facto relationship

Initial contributions

The wife made claims that at the commencement of the relationship in 2007 she had approximately USD42,000 in her 401(k) account, and USD6,000 in US savings bond certificates which she cashed prior to moving to Australia.
She claimed she also owned a chain of drive-through outlets across State Q and State R, all under the umbrella name “[P Pty Ltd Entity 2]” and conducted through a corporation called P Pty Ltd Entity 1. This company also owned the property upon which one of the outlets was operated. The wife conceded that upon deciding to move to Australia with the husband, this parcel of commercial real estate was sold, and after payment of taxes and repayment of a mortgage, USD9,500 remained. The wife said most of the other businesses were transferred to her ex-husband.
The husband gave evidence that the wife told him she was being pressed by creditors in 2007, although she denied this in cross examination. She claimed the outlets were a healthy business, so healthy in fact that she paid USD12,000 to a franchising consultant who was about to organise a franchise for them. However, no franchise ever eventuated and it emerged in cross examination that the financial health of these businesses was open to question.
There was no clear evidence of the individual or combined value of the outlets in 2007. Such evidence as is available does not support an inference of any significant value. The surplus of funds which accrued to the wife after the sale of the commercial real estate was modest. The wife tendered a profit or loss statement for P Pty Ltd Entity 1 for the financial year 2006 which showed the company ran at a loss of about -$5,000.
I am not satisfied the wife gave up a valuable group of businesses to move to Australia in 2007. However, I find that the wife gave up access and entitlements to a financial resource, which had some value, partly to satisfy her ex-husband and partly to move to Australia.
There was no dispute that the husband brought substantial assets to the relationship.
He claimed his assets at cohabitation in December 2007 had a total value of $5,161,259. In cross examination he conceded this total was overstated because it did not take into account some liabilities or that at the time Suburb T was jointly owned with his ex-wife, and included items such as Motor Vehicle 3 with a value of $50,000 based upon nothing other than a representation by his son of a sale price of $25,000 in 2019. I accept the total value of $5,161,259 is an overstatement.
However, even allowing for overstatement, I am satisfied the husband’s initial financial contributions far exceeded those of the wife. The value of his interest in the commercial centre as at December 2007 was about $4,000,000.

Financial contributions during the relationship

The wife did not work during the relationship. The wife claims this was because the husband did not want her to work. The husband claimed the wife chose not to work, but he tried to help her to find work. I do not accept the wife’s argument in this regard. I deal with this issue below under s 90SM(4)(ca).
There was no dispute that during the relationship the husband was retired but his income paid for the parties’ living expenses, lifestyle and extensive travel. The income was derived from the commercial centre partnership. This asset produced the income which funded the expenses and lifestyle of the parties during the relationship, enabled the purchase of Town K and indirectly the purchase of City G, and was fundamental to any wealth of the parties created during the relationship.
In relation to Town K, I accept the husband paid the entire purchase price and agreed to the wife becoming a co-owner. However, when he purchased her interest for USD150,000, the wife became the owner of those monies in exchange for her interest in Town K.
In relation to City G, I am satisfied the wife paid all but USD40,000 of the purchase price of USD189,499, using the funds received from the purchase of her interest in Town K, while the husband contributed USD40,000 and USD50,960 to the furnishing and household contents of the property.
I accept the wife was able to make her payment towards the purchase price because the husband had earlier purchased her interest in Town K. I also accept that the husband paid the full purchase price for Town K. But the agreement struck between the parties when they separated in 2012 reflected their understanding and assumption that they were co-owners of Town K. It can be inferred from the purchase of the wife’s interest to resolve a property settlement at the point of separation in 2012 that the husband recognised the wife’s interest in Town K as her entitlement at that point in time. The husband made no argument that the wife held her interest in Town K on resulting trust for him, leaving aside any question of a presumption of advancement which no one mentioned.
So the correct analysis seems to be that the husband contributed the entire Town K property during the relationship, and the wife contributed the majority of the purchase price for City G.

Financial contributions post-separation

The husband continued to receive substantial income from the commercial centre after separation. He paid the wife USD24,000 for living expenses. He has continued to pay all outgoings of City G such as taxes, insurance and associated fees, while the wife has had the benefit of living there. The husband has paid all outgoings of Town K. In or around January 2026, the husband drew down $190,000 from an ANZ home loan account secured against Town K to comply with orders for payment to the wife, of which her $150,000 in interim property settlement remained unspent at the date of trial.

(b) the contribution (other than a financial contribution) made directly or indirectly by or on behalf of a party to the de facto relationship

(c) the contribution made by a party to the de facto relationship to the welfare of the family constituted by the parties to the de facto relationship and any children of the de facto relationship, including any contribution made in the capacity of homemaker or parent;

The wife emphasised her role as homemaker in the relationship, cooking, cleaning and organising the common residence. The husband claimed he also made similar contributions particularly cooking, and pointed out that the parties had the benefit of a cleaner during the relationship. I am satisfied the wife made the greater non-financial contributions.

(ca) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the de facto relationship has subjected or exposed the other party, on the ability of a party to the de facto relationship to make the kind of contributions referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c);

The wife gave some evidence which was directed to a submission that she suffered family violence from the husband during the relationship, including in the circumstances surrounding the transfer of City G, discussed above. I will not repeat those matters here.
She claimed the husband was aggressive towards her on numerous occasions when drinking. However, she gave few specific examples, and I am not persuaded this factual assertion is established.
Part of this claim was expressed as coercive control by the husband in denying the wife financial autonomy and preventing her from undertaking paid employment because he wanted her to spend time with him and in particular, to travel extensively together. However, in cross examination the wife conceded the husband supported her desire to find work, he paid for her to attend courses, she volunteered for a short time, and he supported her idea to open a shop in Australia. She conceded that the decision to not work was at least partly her own. The wife argued that the husband’s desire for travel meant she was unable to engage in paid employment, either full or part-time. But it was also undisputed that the parties travelled each year between 2008 and 2019 to the USA at the husband’s expense, for periods of two to six months. This travel provided the wife an opportunity at least to spend time with family and friends if she wished, despite her denials in cross examination, and did not exclude part time employment. I am not persuaded any coercive or controlling behaviour of the husband prevented the wife from undertaking paid work in Australia.
Similarly, I am not persuaded the husband was financially controlling. The wife accepted the husband provided her with a debit card connected to one of his bank accounts which had no limit on spending. She agreed he did not prevent her spending, but claimed she felt constrained in some way. Her argument was that she had no bank account of her own, although it was clear she had a NAB account in 2013, and that she had no knowledge of what was in the husband’s bank accounts. These contentions are unpersuasive. It was undisputed the husband funded the parties’ lifestyle during the relationship. He bestowed financial largesse upon the wife in circumstances where she chose not to work. In summary, the wife purports to invert largesse and financial support into a form of coercive control. The wife presented as an articulate person, who on her own case had many years of commercial experience, with some success, and her own agency in life. Her argument amounted to contending she was a victim, not a beneficiary, of the husband’s largesse. I reject the argument that the husband was financially controlling.
I am not satisfied the husband engaged in family violence which had any effect on the ability of the wife to make her contributions.

(d) the effect of any proposed order upon the earning capacity of either party to the de facto relationship;

The proposed orders will not affect the earning capacity of the husband who, as mentioned, is retired and may assist the wife in returning to some paid work.

(f) any other order made under this Act affecting a party to the de facto relationship or a child of the de facto relationship;

I have considered the interim payment to the wife made in February 2026.

(g) any child support under the Child Support (Assessment) Act 1989 that a party to the de facto relationship has provided for a child of the de facto relationship.

This is not relevant.

ASSESSMENT OF CONTRIBUTIONS

The wife sought a contribution assessment of 25 per cent to her and 75 per cent to the husband.
It is clear the husband made the overwhelming financial contributions both initially and during the relationship, and even after separation. I have given material weight to the homemaker contributions of the wife. Taking a holistic approach, I assess the contributions of the parties overall up to the date of trial 12 per cent to the wife and 88 per cent to the husband.

SECTION 90SM(5) ADJUSTMENT

The Act requires me to take into account the matters referred to in s 90SM(5) of the Act, so far as they are relevant, when considering what orders should be made in these proceedings. As disclosed in the arguments of the parties, the following matters are relevant.

(a) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the de facto relationship (the subject de facto relationship) has subjected or exposed the other party, on the current and future circumstances of the other party, including on any of the matters mentioned elsewhere in this subsection;

I have rejected the wife’s claims of family violence above. Accordingly, I am unable to conclude any family violence has had any effect on the wife.

(b) the age and state of health of each of the parties to the subject de facto relationship;

The wife is 64 years of age. I accept she has had health problems including surgery.
The husband is 74 years of age. He has cancer and suffers from depression and anxiety.

(c) the income, property and financial resources of each of the parties to the subject de facto relationship and the physical and mental capacity of each of them for appropriate gainful employment;

I have already discussed the property of the parties extensively and will not repeat myself.
I give discussion here of two items which appeared on the balance sheet as “financial resources”. The first was the residue of the wife’s 401(k) account with a value of $643. It could have been included as an asset of the wife but the de minimis value makes it immaterial. As explained above, the substantial credit balance in the 401(k) account which existed in 2007 was progressively spent during the relationship.
The second is a potential claim in negligence enjoyed by the husband arising from the setting aside of the BFA. This could be treated as a chose in action, but neither party argued for this to be included on the balance sheet as an asset with a defined value, and neither party suggested this was a financial resource which could be given a value.
The BFA was drafted by a solicitor engaged by the husband, but the wife was required to receive independent advice from a legal practitioner about its terms and the advantages and disadvantages to her (s 90UJ(1)(b) of the Act). It is necessary to emphasise that the basis for setting aside the BFA was not the consequence of the drafting of its terms, but because there was insufficient evidence that the wife had received the legal advice required by s 90UJ(1)(b). This was because she consulted a chamber registrar in the Local Court who was not a registered legal practitioner, but who signed the certificate of advice as a “legally qualified chamber registrar”. There was no evidence from the chamber registrar in the hearing before Aldridge J. On the basis of the certificate, he was prepared to accept the wife received some advice from the chamber registrar about the content of the BFA, but he was unable to infer the wife received advice about the terms of the BFA and the advantages and disadvantages of them for the wife (Ericsson & Rogers at [43]–[47]).
In those circumstances it is unclear who would be the proposed defendant in any claim for negligence by the husband, or what breach of duty would be claimed. The question of damages is equally unclear because the basic questions of causation and quantification will be significantly impacted by the outcome in these proceedings. In particular, it is not self evident that the husband would be able to recover as damages in any negligence claim, an amount equivalent to the value of the property transfer (City G) and cash payment which I propose to order in these proceedings. Consequently, while I have considered the possible negligence claim, it is not possible to give it much weight. Neither party made any compelling submission to the contrary.
The wife currently works part time in her own business, “[D Company]”. The business was not included on the balance sheet and there was no suggestion it had significant value. She said she will try to take on more work once proceedings have concluded. I accept her health problems, in light of her age, are likely to erode her stamina for some types of work. I find she retains some capacity for paid employment, but she provided no medical evidence which could allow a clearer or more certain conclusion about her future capacity.
The husband’s age and health mean he is most unlikely to engage in paid work, and he has in any event been retired for many years.

(e) any liabilities incurred by either of the parties to the subject de facto relationship or both of them, including the nature of the liabilities and the circumstances relating to them;

The wife has no liabilities of any significance on the balance sheet. The husband’s interest in the commercial centre is subject to a commercial loan which he will continue to be liable for jointly with his partners, although the commercial centre generates the income sufficient to service this loan. He has also drawn a loan secured against Town K to make the payment in February 2026 to the wife in compliance with court orders.
I propose to order the husband to cause a transfer of the City G property to the wife. Once this is achieved she will become liable for taxes, insurances and maintenance of that property.

(w) any other fact or circumstance which, in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account.

The wife submitted that the financial disparity between the parties was stark, which supports a greater adjustment in her favour. There is no doubt the disparity is stark. But it arises most obviously from the increases in value of the commercial centre partnership assets and, to a lesser extent, Town K. The evidence does not demonstrate that during the relationship, these increases in value were the result of efforts or contributions by either party (Whiton & Dagne [2019] FamCAFC 192; (2019) 60 Fam LR 170 at [34]). In relation to the commercial centre, the evidence did not, and probably could not, show the increase in value of the partnership assets was the result of the efforts of the partners or simple market forces, or some combination of both.
I take account here of a liability of the wife for legal fees, rather than under s 90SM(5)(e), because there was no clear evidence about the amount owing by the wife or an agreed position about the treatment of unpaid legal fees. There was no information about the husband’s legal fees. In final submissions it was claimed the wife will have to pay $77,000 to Z Pty Ltd, a litigation funder, $72,000 to her previous solicitor and an expected $88,200 for the final hearing, a total of about $237,000. However, in the wife’s evidence there was a Z Pty Ltd account statement dated 28 February 2026, showing a credit limit of $150,000, funds advanced of $74,441.64, and a current repayment amount of $83,026. These liabilities were not included in the balance sheet. Since the figures are not agreed or all clearly demonstrated in the evidence, I will take account of the broad factual proposition that the wife will have to pay some legal fees.

Conclusion

Weighing the matters discussed above, I conclude an adjustment in the wife’s favour of 4 per cent is indicated.

JUST AND EQUITABLE ORDERS

The assets of the parties should be divided 16 per cent to the wife and 84 per cent to the husband. On the basis of the agreed values on the balance sheet, this can be achieved by a transfer of City G to the wife together with a cash payment.
Although the parties agree that it would be just and equitable to make an order adjusting their property interests, s 90SM(2) requires the Court to be satisfied the proposed order itself is just and equitable.
The High Court in Stanford commented at [36] on the meaning of “just and equitable” as follows:
The expression "just and equitable" is a qualitative description of a conclusion reached after examination of a range of potentially competing considerations. It does not admit of exhaustive definition. It is not possible to chart its metes and bounds. ...

(Footnotes omitted)
The Act confers a discretion which, provided the required matters are taken into account, does not dictate a particular method of formulating an appropriate order (Norbis at 533–534). It has been recognised that the application of percentages does not necessarily result in a just and equitable outcome; the real impact in money terms is the critical issue (JEL and DDF (2001) FLC 93-075 at [139]–[140]). It is the ultimate order which must be just and equitable, not the underlying assessment of contributions (Keehan & Keehan [2015] FamCAFC 122 at [78]).
In this matter, the wife proposed a percentage division, while the husband concentrated more specifically on selling City G and dividing the proceeds. In closing argument, the wife maintained the parties’ property should be divided 35 per cent to her, but specified this would require a payment of $1,750,000 plus the transfer of City G. I am satisfied a percentage division is appropriate. However, her claim does not accord with my conclusions on the appropriate percentage division.
I note also that the wife could not point to a source for a large payment such as $1,750,000, other than contending the husband could resign as a partner in the commercial centre or wind up the partnership and use his interest to satisfy the claims of the wife. It was common ground the partnership agreement provided no mechanism for dissolving the partnership, and the husband would be required to resort to the provisions of the Partnership Act 1892 (NSW) if the partners did not agree to the basis of his withdrawal and the value of his interest. Such an outcome would potentially have a significant impact upon the husband’s partners. The wife made no particular submission explaining why this would be just and equitable, especially in circumstances where she made no direct contribution to the husband’s interest in the partnership, which he held for many years before the parties met. He is also 74 years of age, in poor health, and the partnership is his only source of income in retirement.
However, I consider that the husband should be compelled to transfer City G to the wife. She requires a place to live and has been living in City G. As already discussed, I have found she paid the majority of the purchase price for City G and the husband holds the view that the wife is a co-owner. The transfer can be achieved by making an order binding the husband in personam to take the necessary steps to do so in the USA, with a provision for the charging of Town K as security and its sale in the event of default.
On the basis of a division of 16 per cent to the wife, based on the values agreed on the balance sheet, the wife is also entitled to a cash payment of $416,121, to receive her entitlement. It is likely the husband will be required to obtain finance to make this payment. The husband should therefore be given a reasonably long period of time to raise this amount.
On a 16/84 percentage division, the wife and husband will have the assets and liabilities as set out in the below table (figures rounded).

COSTS

My preliminary view is that there should be no order as to costs of either party in these proceedings. However, I will order that any party who seeks costs to file the relevant application within 28 days of the date of pronouncing orders, in default of which there shall be no order as to costs.

CONCLUSION

For all the foregoing reasons I am satisfied the orders set out at the commencement of these reasons should be made.
